source.title: A contextual binding theory of episodic memory: systems consolidation reconsidered

confidence_rationale: Theoretical framework that reinterprets multi-trace persistence as ongoing hippocampal contextual binding. Provides clean theoretical home for MECH-272 routing as context-dependent retrieval and reframes MECH-284 V_s residual as contextual interference rather than consolidation-readiness. Mapping depends on accepting a contested theory.
failure_signatures: If standard systems consolidation is correct, the V_s-as-contextual-mismatch reading needs adjustment -- V_s residual would then be a consolidation-readiness signal rather than an interference measure.
Theoretical framework only -- no new empirical evidence; specific predictions about hippocampal lesion impairment of old memories remain mixed in the literature.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Episodic memory remains hippocampus-dependent across the lifespan because hippocampus binds item to context. Forgetting is contextual interference, not failed transfer. Sleep reduces interference rather than transferring traces to neocortex.
mapping.ree_translation: Dual-trace anchors at MECH-269 are theoretically grounded as persistent hippocampal contextual bindings. MECH-272 routing maps to context-dependent retrieval. MECH-284 V_s residual is a measure of contextual interference per anchor, accumulated during waking; MECH-285 sleep-priority then sharpens context-tagging rather than moving traces.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Theoretical review (no new empirical data); contextual-binding theory is contested vs standard systems consolidation. Specific scale (place vs schema vs event-segment) of binding is unspecified.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical synthesis of behavioural, lesion, neuroimaging, and sleep literature on episodic memory consolidation.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality. Mapping fidelity moderate because architectural translation is theoretically clean but rests on a contested theoretical framework.
